raw_case:
Untreated ALCAPA diagnosed in gestational ultrasonography
ALCAPA is a rare congenital anomaly that presents with left ventricular (LV) dysfunction and mitral valve regurgitation. The mortality rate is roughly 90% if the intracoronary collateral isn't significantly augmented. Malignant arrhythmias resulting in sudden death are common, affecting nearly 90% of patients with a mean age of 35 years. Especially during pregnancy, untreated ALCAPA can lead to high mortality and complications. Pregnant women with congenital heart disease have high risks for both themselves and their fetuses. Therefore, screening for congenital heart disease is very important in early diagnosis, counseling, and management. Most women born with congenital heart disease (CHD) will reach reproductive age.We report a case of a 30-year-old woman in her second trimester of pregnancy who presents with ALCAPA, preserved ejection fraction (EF), and mild LV dilation. An uncommon feature, in this case, is the origin of the left main (LM) coronary artery from the posterior to the right of the main pulmonary artery (MPA), which is very close to the ascending aorta, mimicking the normal origin of the LM from the aortic root in 2D transthoracic echocardiography. The patient also has preserved left ventricular function with endocardial fibroelastosis. Echocardiography should be performed every 4 weeks to assess the progression of the disease during pregnancy.

Introduction

Echocardiography plays an important role in the detection of ALCAPA. Early diagnosis helps improve the prognosis of the patient through timely surgical intervention, aiming to restore the anatomy and physiology of the coronary system, preserve LV function, and avoid sudden cardiac death during exertion, especially in the gestation period. Critical echocardiographic findings include dilation of the right coronary artery, absence of the left coronary artery (LCA) ostium in the aortic root, continuous color flow in the MPA above the pulmonary valve, and collaterals within the ventricular septum and free ventricular wall, along with left ventricular (LV) dysfunction and mitral valve regurgitation [1,2]. Multislice computed tomography can accurately identify the abnormal coronary artery origin but is harmful during gestation. Magnetic resonance imaging is also valuable for diagnosing ALCAPA. Various imaging modalities have been described, which help diagnose ALCAPA, but differences in echocardiographic features can lead to missed diagnoses [3].The case report showed uncommon features, including significant dilation of the LCA and an uncommon origin of the LM from the posterior to the right of the MPA, which is very close to the ascending aorta, mimicking the normal origin of the LM from the aortic root in 2D transthoracic echocardiography. The patient also had preserved LV function with endocardial fibroelastosis. Hemodynamics change during pregnancy due to disturbances in heart structure, especially abnormal coronary vasculature. There are major increases in cardiac output and a decrease in maternal systemic vascular resistance; the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system is significantly activated, and the heart and vasculature undergo remodeling [4], resulting in an increased risk of cardiac events and mortality.

Case Presentation

A 30-year-old woman in her second trimester (19 weeks) of pregnancy presents with chronic dyspnea on exertion. The patient has been experiencing worsening left-sided chest heaviness and was subsequently referred to our hospital. She has no medical history of coronary risk factors and no family history of premature coronary artery disease or congenital heart conditions. Physical examination was normal, and a heart murmur was not recorded. ECG showed sinus rhythm without ST or T wave changes. Obstetric ultrasound showed normal amniotic volume with a heart rate of 146 bpm, and positive fetal movement.Echocardiography showed mild LV dilation with preserved EF, without mitral valve regurgitation, and endocardial fibroelastosis (Fig. 1A). Collaterals were observed within the ventricular septum and free ventricular wall (Fig. 1B). The origin of the LM was from the posterior to the right of the MPA, very close to the ascending aorta, mimicking the normal origin of the LM from the aortic root (Fig. 2A, B). There were no structural abnormalities of the aortic, mitral, tricuspid, or pulmonic valves, and there was very mild mitral regurgitation by Doppler. We will continue to follow the patient's pregnancy every 4 weeks to check echocardiography and obstetric Doppler.

Discussion

The adult patient presents with signs of chest discomfort and dyspnea on exertion due to increased flow from coronary steal, which occurs during stress. Because of the formation of collateral circulation, patients with ALCAPA can be asymptomatic in the early stages. However, as the disease progresses, cardiac structural changes such as mitral valve regurgitation occur. There are different proposed mechanisms, including left ventricular myocardial infarction, scarring, calcification of papillary muscles, and endocardial fibroelastosis, which result in varying degrees of fibrosis in myocardial tissue and increase the risk of fatal ventricular tachyarrhythmias. Physiological changes in the cardiovascular system during pregnancy may pose a risk for those with congenital heart disease who are not able to sufficiently adapt [5]. Hypertensive disorders are the most frequent cardiovascular disorders during pregnancy, occurring in 5%-10% of all pregnancies. Moreover, pregnancy is a hypercoagulable state associated with an increased risk of thromboembolism. This situation, combined with ALCAPA, results in an increased risk of pulmonary embolism and myocardial infarction during pregnancy. The left ventricular adaptation to pregnancy can be suboptimal in heart disease. Maternal cardiac dysfunction is related to impaired uteroplacental flow and suboptimal fetal outcomes. The risk estimation needs to be re-evaluated during each pregnancy visit, as the risk of complications may change over time. Natriuretic peptide levels are associated with the occurrence of cardiac events, with N-terminal pro-B-type natriuretic peptide (NT-proBNP) levels greater than 128 pg/mL at 20 weeks of pregnancy being predictive of events later in the pregnancy [6]. Pre-eclampsia is associated with heart failure in women with heart disease. Transthoracic echocardiography is a method to diagnose ALCAPA, but in this case, there is an uncommon feature: dilation of both the coronary artery and the origin of the LM from the posterior to the right of the MPA, which is very close to the ascending aorta, mimicking the normal origin of the LM from the aortic root. Additionally, there is mild LV dilation, which can mimic a coronary fistula, but the right ventricle and pulmonary artery are not dilated.In women with a moderate or high risk of complications during pregnancy, the minimum team requirements are a cardiologist, obstetrician, and anesthetist, all with expertise in managing high-risk pregnancies in women with heart disease. Additional experts may be involved depending on the individual situation. The patient should have an echocardiogram every 4 weeks to assess the progression of the disease, including left ventricular function and the mitral valve, and to develop a clear plan for labor and the early postpartum period [7]. The timing of delivery depends on the weighing of maternal, obstetric, and fetal risks. The ACOG recommends elective induction of labor for pregnant women with cardiac abnormalities between 39 and 40 weeks of gestation if they do not go into labor spontaneously [8]. SPECT has been shown to detect ischemia in adult patients with untreated ALCAPA. Preoperative demonstration of ischemia is required, given that the myocardium cannot recover from this lesion [9].

Conclusions

ALCAPA has a high mortality rate in adult patients. The uncommon feature of echo misdiagnosis results in delayed surgery. Direct transfer and reimplantation of the anomalous coronary artery into the aorta is the preferred surgical technique. Especially in pregnant women, increased cardiac events lead to higher mortality. Follow-up every 4 weeks with echocardiography is necessary to assess the progression of the disease.

Patient consent

The following information must be provided for this form to be processed accurately.•I hereby give my consent for images or other clinical information relating to my case to be reported in a medical publication.•I understand that my name and initials will not be published and that efforts will be made to conceal my identity, but that anonymity cannot be guaranteed.•I understand that the material may be published in a journal, Web site or other form of publication. As a result, I understand that the material may be seen by the general public.•I understand that the material may be included in medical books. A statement indicating: “Written informed consent was obtained from the patient to publish this report by the journal's patient consent policy”.

Availability of data and materials

Data and materials used and/or analyzed during the current study are available from the corresponding author upon reasonable request.

Ethics approval

Our institution does not require ethical approval for reporting individual cases or case series.

generate_case.case_summary:
- Patient Information: 30-year-old woman, second trimester of pregnancy (19 weeks).
- Chief Complaint: Chronic dyspnea on exertion, worsening left-sided chest heaviness.
- History of Present Illness: None beyond presenting symptoms.
- Past Medical History: None reported.
- Personal History: No coronary risk factors.
- Family History: No history of premature coronary artery disease or congenital heart conditions.
- Physical Examination: Normal; no heart murmur.
- Ancillary Tests:
  - ECG: Sinus rhythm, no ST or T wave changes.
  - Obstetric Ultrasound: Normal amniotic volume, fetal heartbeat at 146 bpm, positive fetal movement.
  - Echocardiography: 
    - Mild left ventricular (LV) dilation with preserved ejection fraction (EF).
    - Endocardial fibroelastosis.
    - Collaterals within the ventricular septum and free ventricular wall.
    - Left main (LM) coronary artery originating from an unusual position (posterior to the right of the main pulmonary artery, close to the ascending aorta).
    - Very mild mitral valve regurgitation.
    - No structural valvular abnormalities.
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
- **Initial Differential Diagnosis:**
  - **ALCAPA (Anomalous Left Coronary Artery from the Pulmonary Artery):** Strongly suspected due to the echocardiographic findings of abnormal LM origin, collaterals, mild LV dilation, and endocardial fibroelastosis. 
  - **Coronary Artery Fistula:** Considered due to dilation and unusual coronary anatomy but less likely because the right ventricle and pulmonary artery were not dilated.
  - **Structural Heart Disease with LV Dilation:** Unlikely due to normal valve structure and preserved EF without signs of ischemia, calcification, or typical cardiac remodeling.
  
- **Rationale for Exclusion of Other Conditions:**
  - Coronary artery fistula was excluded due to the absence of dilation in the pulmonary artery and right ventricle.
  - Generic structural heart disease or ischemic cardiomyopathy was excluded based on the lack of ischemic features on ECG and preserved EF.
  - ALCAPA was confirmed by the unique echocardiographic findings showing abnormal LM origin, collaterals, and fibroelastosis even in the absence of overt mitral valve regurgitation or severe LV dysfunction.
generate_case.final_diagnosis: Anomalous Left Coronary Artery from the Pulmonary Artery (ALCAPA) in a pregnant adult with mild LV dilation, preserved EF, and endocardial fibroelastosis. Diagnosis was confirmed based on distinctive echocardiographic signs.
generate_case.diagnosis_results: ALCAPA (Anomalous Left Coronary Artery from the Pulmonary Artery)

results.input:
Please carefully study the following patient case summary, conduct a comprehensive and in-depth diagnostic analysis, and clearly provide the final diagnosis result.

- Patient Information: 30-year-old woman, second trimester of pregnancy (19 weeks).
- Chief Complaint: Chronic dyspnea on exertion, worsening left-sided chest heaviness.
- History of Present Illness: None beyond presenting symptoms.
- Past Medical History: None reported.
- Personal History: No coronary risk factors.
- Family History: No history of premature coronary artery disease or congenital heart conditions.
- Physical Examination: Normal; no heart murmur.
- Ancillary Tests:
  - ECG: Sinus rhythm, no ST or T wave changes.
  - Obstetric Ultrasound: Normal amniotic volume, fetal heartbeat at 146 bpm, positive fetal movement.
  - Echocardiography: 
    - Mild left ventricular (LV) dilation with preserved ejection fraction (EF).
    - Endocardial fibroelastosis.
    - Collaterals within the ventricular septum and free ventricular wall.
    - Left main (LM) coronary artery originating from an unusual position (posterior to the right of the main pulmonary artery, close to the ascending aorta).
    - Very mild mitral valve regurgitation.
    - No structural valvular abnormalities.

---

**Format to Follow:**

```

...
<step n> Specific thinking content of this step

```
results.content:
<think>
Okay, let's try to figure out this patient's condition. So, the patient is a 30-year-old woman in her second trimester of pregnancy. She's experiencing chronic dyspnea on exertion and worsening left-sided chest heaviness. The physical exam is normal, no heart murmur. The ECG is normal with sinus rhythm and no ST or T wave changes. The obstetric ultrasound shows everything's okay with the fetus. The echocardiography findings are a bit more complex.

First, the echocardiography shows mild left ventricular dilation with preserved ejection fraction. That's interesting. Preserved EF suggests that the heart is still pumping adequately, but there's some dilation. Then there's endocardial fibroelastosis. I remember that endocardial fibroelastosis is a condition where there's abnormal thickening of the endocardium, which can be congenital. It's sometimes associated with other heart defects or conditions like Noonan syndrome or other genetic disorders. But the patient has no family history of congenital heart issues, so maybe that's not it.

Next, there are collaterals within the ventricular septum and free ventricular wall. Collateral vessels are usually a sign of some kind of obstruction, like in coronary artery disease. But the patient is young, has no risk factors, and the family history is negative for coronary artery disease. However, the coronary artery origin is unusual—left main (LM) coronary artery is posterior to the right of the main pulmonary artery, close to the ascending aorta. That's an anomalous origin. Anomalous coronary arteries can be a problem, especially if they cause compression or reduced blood flow. But in this case, the patient is in her second trimester, and the symptoms are related to pregnancy. 

Pregnancy increases cardiac output and places more demand on the heart. If there's an anomalous coronary artery, maybe the unusual origin is causing some sort of ischemia, especially during exertion. But the ejection fraction is preserved, and there's no ST changes on ECG, which might suggest that there's no acute ischemia. However, the endocardial fibroelastosis could be contributing to diastolic dysfunction or some form of restrictive physiology. 

The very mild mitral regurgitation is probably not the main issue here. The key findings are the anomalous coronary artery origin, left ventricular dilation, and endocardial fibroelastosis. Endocardial fibroelastosis can be part of a congenital heart condition. Maybe this is a case of a congenital anomaly with associated coronary artery origin. 

Wait, could this be a case of left ventricular noncompaction? No, because the echocardiogram mentions endocardial fibroelastosis, not noncompaction. Another possibility is a myopathy or some form of cardiomyopathy. However, the presence of collaterals and the anomalous coronary artery might suggest that there's a chronic issue with blood supply leading to compensatory changes like LV dilation and fibrosis.

Putting it all together, the combination of anomalous coronary artery origin, endocardial fibroelastosis, and LV dilation with preserved EF might point towards a rare congenital condition. Maybe a form of coronary artery anomaly with secondary myocardial changes. However, since the patient is pregnant, the dyspnea could also be related to the increased cardiac workload of pregnancy, but the chest heaviness is more concerning. 

Another angle: endocardial fibroelastosis is sometimes associated with endocarditis, but the patient has no history of infection or fever. Also, the physical exam is normal. The collaterals might be a response to chronic ischemia due to the anomalous coronary artery. If the left main artery is in an unusual position, maybe it's compressed or has reduced blood flow, leading to ischemia and subsequent fibrosis. 

So, the final diagnosis might be a combination of anomalous left main coronary artery origin with endocardial fibroelastosis and mild left ventricular dilation. But the exact name of the condition? Maybe a congenital coronary anomaly with secondary myocardial changes. Alternatively, could this be a case of a rare congenital heart defect that includes these features? Or perhaps it's a form of restrictive cardiomyopathy due to endocardial fibroelastosis. However, the presence of collaterals suggests chronic ischemia, so the diagnosis might be coronary artery anomaly with secondary endocardial fibroelastosis and left ventricular dilation.
</think>

```
### Resoning:
<step 1> The patient presents with dyspnea on exertion and left-sided chest heaviness during the second trimester of pregnancy. While pregnancy can increase cardiac workload, the absence of risk factors, family history, and structural valvular abnormalities suggests a cardiac pathology rather than physiological changes.

<step 2> Echocardiography reveals mild left ventricular (LV) dilation with preserved ejection fraction, which may indicate compensatory remodeling. Endocardial fibroelastosis, a rare condition characterized by abnormal endocardial thickening, is noted. This finding is often associated with congenital heart disease, restrictive cardiomyopathies, or metabolic disorders.

<step 3> The presence of collaterals in the ventricular septum and free wall suggests chronic ischemia or altered coronary perfusion. The anomalous origin of the left main (LM) coronary artery (posterior to the right of the main pulmonary artery, near the ascending aorta) is a critical finding. Such anomalies can lead to myocardial ischemia, especially during exertion, and may explain the dyspnea and chest heaviness.

<step 4> The combination of anomalous coronary artery origin, endocardial fibroelastosis, and LV dilation with preserved EF points to a rare congenital cardiac anomaly. The collaterals likely represent a compensatory response to chronic ischemia from the anomalous coronary artery, leading to myocardial fibrosis and remodeling.

<step 5> Given the absence of acute ischemic changes on ECG and the lack of structural valvular disease, the diagnosis is likely a congenital anomaly involving the LM coronary artery with secondary endocardial fibroelastosis and LV dysfunction.

### Answer:
Congenital anomalous origin of the left main coronary artery with secondary endocardial fibroelastosis and mild left ventricular dilation.
```
